summary.brief: Santamaria v. Cleveland Clinic Found.. Medical malpractice case involving surgery general.

evidence_index.text: Santamaria was approximately 67 years old at the time and suffered diabetes, high blood pressure, and several lower urinary-tract conditions including nocturia
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The official diagnosis was benign prostatic hyperplasia (“BPH”), a common condition in older men in which the prostrate is enlarged and obstructs the flow of urine
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Canterbury discovered a preexisting condition at the time of the initial diagnosis, described as an extremely rare condition. Santamaria’s bladder had become displaced and protruded into his scrotum.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: After the hernia surgery, sometime in mid-2018, Dr. Canterbury began discussing the next steps to treating the enlarged prostate.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He recommended a transurethral resection of the prostrate; commonly referred to as a “TURP” for short.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In executing the TURP procedure, there is no specific amount of prostatic tissue to be removed; the amount removed is case specific.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It is undisputed that Dr. Canterbury took a limited approach in performing the TURP and removed a small amount of prostatic tissue around the bladder neck
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: After the procedure, however, Santamaria suffered known complications. He developed urinary tract infections and blood clots
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Wright testified to finding no correlation between the amount of tissue removed and abatement of symptoms from BPH.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Wright

evidence_index.text: Dr. Wright nonetheless concluded that Dr. Canterbury’s approach met the minimum standard of care for performing the button TURP procedure.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Wright

evidence_index.text: Dr. Babayan testified that in 27 percent of cases involving the TURP procedure, the patient needs to undergo a second TURP procedure to remove additional tissue.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Babayan

evidence_index.text: Dr. Steinberg, “[T]he standard of care is to remove the appropriate amount of tissue to relieve the obstruction so the urine will flow out at the end of the procedure.”
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Steinberg

evidence_index.text: The jury considered that evidence and rendered a verdict in favor of Cleveland Clinic Foundation and Brian T. Canterbury, M.D.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The standard of care, as the jury was charged in this case, is whether the physician exercised his professional judgment in the given circumstance
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: The decision of the trial court is affirmed, and the jury’s unanimous conclusion in favor of the Cleveland Clinic Foundation and Dr. Canterbury cannot
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient experienced nocturia and incomplete bladder emptying. Diagnosed with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) and a rare condition where the bladder protruded into the scrotum.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Benign prostatic hyperplasia with bladder herniation

requestables.name: Urology Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation for BPH and bladder herniation

reveal.decision_impact: Proceed with TURP after hernia repair

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with urinary symptoms and is diagnosed with BPH and bladder herniation

key_events.event: Diagnosis of BPH and bladder herniation

timeline_phases.name: TURP Procedure
timeline_phases.description: Transurethral resection of the prostate performed

key_events.event: TURP procedure performed with limited tissue removal

timeline_phases.name: Post-Procedure Follow-up
timeline_phases.description: Patient experiences complications and seeks second opinion

key_events.event: Patient develops urinary tract infections and blood clots

decision_points.prompt: How much prostatic tissue should be removed during TURP?

options.description: Remove sufficient tissue to ensure unobstructed urine flow
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires removal of enough tissue to relieve obstruction

options.description: Remove minimal tissue around bladder neck
options.clinical_reasoning: Conservative approach to minimize risk of incontinence

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant removed minimal tissue around bladder neck
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Based on patient's other conditions and prior hernia repair

expected_action_court.description: Remove sufficient tissue to ensure unobstructed urine flow
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated more tissue should have been removed

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Insufficient tissue removal led to continued symptoms
explanation.what_should_have_happened: More tissue should have been removed to relieve obstruction
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient required a second TURP procedure to relieve symptoms
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Resolution of symptoms after second procedure

key_findings: Dr. Canterbury's approach met the minimum standard of care
Jury found in favor of the Cleveland Clinic Foundation

complications.complication: Urinary tract infections and blood clots

allegations.allegation: Failure to remove sufficient prostatic tissue during TURP

court_findings.finding: Dr. Canterbury met the minimum standard of care
